text.title: Elicitation Techniques — Examples and Antipatterns
text.summary: Requirements discovery interview example, process mapping workshop example, and common elicitation antipatterns.

example.note: Stakeholder: Customer Service Manager. Objective: understand current pain points and information needs.

Questions: (1) "Walk me through a typical customer inquiry." [process understanding — open] (2) "What are the most common issues your team faces?" [pain points — open] (3) "What information do you wish you had access to?" [needs — probing] (4) "How do you measure success?" [metrics — closed] (5) "What would ideal look like?" [vision — open]

Insights gathered: average call takes 12 minutes (7 minutes searching for info); 3 different systems checked per call; no visibility into order status; customer satisfaction declining. Each insight becomes a candidate requirement with this session_id as source citation.
Objective: map current order fulfillment process. Participants: order entry, warehouse, shipping, customer service teams.
Activities: each team presents their steps (15 min each) → group maps end-to-end flow (45 min) → identify handoff points and issues (20 min) → dot-vote to prioritize pain points (15 min).
Output: current state process map, list of 12 pain points, top 3 priorities. Each pain point becomes a candidate requirement citing this workshop session_id and the team that raised it.

antipattern.description: Leading questions in interview guides — "Don't you think the system is too slow?" or "Wouldn't it be better if...?"
antipattern.reason: Leading questions suggest the answer and confirm pre-existing assumptions; they produce requirements that reflect the BA's beliefs, not the stakeholder's actual needs.

antipattern.description: Using prototyping as the first elicitation technique before any interviews or observation.
antipattern.reason: Prototyping anchors stakeholders on the prototype's affordances; they stop imagining alternatives and stop surfacing underlying needs, producing requirements shaped by the prototype rather than by the problem.

antipattern.description: Treating document analysis as sufficient on its own without observation or interviews.
antipattern.reason: Documentation typically lags actual implementation by 6-18 months; as-documented state is not as-implemented state and not where real requirements live.

antipattern.description: Surveys with less than 30% response rate reported as representative data.
antipattern.reason: Low-response surveys are anecdotes with statistical framing; they mislead stakeholders about the breadth of the finding and produce requirements that serve the responding minority.

antipattern.description: Scheduling too many elicitation sessions without preparation — wasting stakeholder time.
antipattern.reason: Stakeholder time is the scarcest resource in elicitation; unprepared sessions burn goodwill and produce shallow answers that require follow-up sessions.

antipattern.description: Single-technique elicitation — relying only on interviews without observation or document analysis.
antipattern.reason: Single-source requirements lack triangulation; stated needs (interviews) differ systematically from actual behavior (observation), and the gap is where the most valuable requirements live.
